|pattern_la3=_sthelens2026t|pattern_b3=_sthelens2026t|pattern_ra3=_sthelens2026t|pattern_sh3=_sthelens2026t|pattern_so3=_sthelens2026t}} '''St Helens R.F.C.''', commonly known as '''Saints''', is a professional [[rugby league]] club in [[St Helens, Merseyside]], [[England]]. Founded in 1873, the club is one of the oldest members of the [[Rugby Football League]], and one of the most successful clubs in its history. The club plays their home games at the [[Totally Wicked Stadium|BrewDog Stadium]] and currently compete in [[Super League]], the top tier of [[British rugby league system]]. Since 1961 the club's home colours have been distinctive white shirts with a prominent red "V" on the chest of the jersey. St Helens have won the [[List of British rugby league champions|League Championship]] 17 times, the [[Challenge Cup]] 13 times and [[World Club Challenge]] on three occasions.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php|title=St Helens RLFC club Records|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 June 2009|access-date=24 June 2009}}{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/club.php?id=33498|title=St Helens RLFC Club Profile|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=15 July 2009|access-date=14 July 2009|archive-date=21 September 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100921105101/http://www.superleague.co.uk/club.php?id=33498|url-status=dead}} Since the foundation of the Super League in 1996, Saints have been the most successful team, winning 10 championships (including 4 in a row between 2019 and 2022), and being runner up a further 5 times (losing to [[Leeds Rhinos]] each time between 2007 and 2011), while winning the [[League Leaders Shield]] 9 times. The club have a traditional [[St Helens R.F.C.–Wigan Warriors rivalry|rivalry with Wigan Warriors]] as the two most successful clubs in the British game, and close regional rivals. The clubs face each other traditionally on [[Good Friday]]. The club also have a local rivalry with the [[Warrington Wolves]] and a rivalry with Leeds Rhinos borne out of close competition during the Super League era. ==History== {{main|History of St Helens R.F.C.}} {{see also|List of St Helens R.F.C. seasons}} ===Early years (1873–1945)=== [[File:Challenge cup 1897.jpg|left|thumb|240px|St. Helens pictured in the [[1896–97 Northern Rugby Football Union season#Final|first ever Challenge Cup Final]], 1897: Batley (left) vs St Helens (right)]] St Helens are the oldest members of the [[Rugby Football League]].{{cite web|url=http://www.rugbyfootballhistory.com/Schism.html|title=The Great Schism|publisher=RugbyFootballHistory.com|date=20 August 1895|access-date=24 December 2009}} Founded as '''St Helens Football Club''' on 19 November 1873 at the Fleece Hotel by William Douglas Herman,{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/clubinfo|title=St Helens RLFC Club History|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=29 August 1873|access-date=14 July 2009|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090722033905/http://www.saintsrlfc.com/clubinfo|archive-date=22 July 2009}} they played their first ever match on 31 January 1874 against [[Liverpool]] Royal Infirmary.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php|title=St Helens First Match|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=31 January 1874|access-date=14 July 2009}} They became known as '''St Helens Rangers''' up until the 1880s. The club moved from the City Road Ground in 1890 where they had shared with [[St Helens Recreation RLFC|St Helens Recs]] when neither were members of the Northern Rugby Football Union. They defeated Manchester Rangers in the first match played at Knowsley Road. In 1895 the club were one of [[History of rugby league#The schism in England|22 clubs that resigned]] from the [[Rugby Football Union]] and established the [[Rugby Football League|Northern Union]]. The first match of the new code was an 8–3 win at home to [[Rochdale Hornets]] before 3,000 spectators, [[Robert Doherty (rugby league)|Bob Doherty]] scoring St Helens' first try.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7|title=Saints' first match in Northern Union|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=7 July 1895|access-date=12 July 2009}} They played in a vertically striped blue and white jersey—a stark contrast to the well known broad red band which would become the kit for the club later. The club reverted to this kit for one season during the rugby league centenary season in [[1994–95 Rugby Football League season|1995]]. The [[Challenge Cup]] was launched in [[1897 in sports#Rugby league|1897]] and it was St Helens who contested its first final with [[Batley Bulldogs|Batley]], at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]], [[Leeds]]. The "Gallant Youths" of Batley emerged victorious 10–3,{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/4158148.stm|title=Challenge Cup Role of Honour|publisher=BBC|date=26 August 2006|access-date=11 July 2009}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=543|title=1897 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=BBC|date=26 August 1897|access-date=11 July 2009}} with Dave "Red" Traynor scoring the lone St Helens' try.{{cite book|last = Hoole| first = Les | title = The Rugby League Challenge Cup | publisher = Breedon Books Ltd | date = 8 August 1998 | id = () | isbn = 1-85983-094-3 }} {| class="toccolours" !{{fontcolor|Red|St Helens 1897 Challenge Cup Final team}} |- |align=centre|

St Helens were placed in Division 1 but finished next to bottom and suffered relegation.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1902%203|title=1902/1903 Division 1 League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1903|access-date=12 July 2009}} Promotion was gained at the first attempt,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1903%204|title=1903/1904 Division 2 League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1904|access-date=12 July 2009}} only for another poor year to see them finish once again in a relegation position.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1904%205|title=1904/1905 Division 1 League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1905|access-date=12 July 2009}} However the two Divisions became one League to save the club from a second relegation.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1905%206|title=1905/1906 Northern Football Union League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1906|access-date=12 July 2009}} The Champion fortunes that St Helens fans' greet today were certainly not apparent in this period, with the club finishing fourth to bottom in 1907,{{cite 1913|access-date=1 August 2010}} On 14 June 1913, St Helens Recs joined the Northern Union after defecting from [[rugby union]] and [[association football]]. The Recs were based individually at the City Road ground, after previously sharing with St Helens, before their move to Knowsley Road, when neither played rugby league. The Recs played their first game on 6 September 1913. St Helens now had two [[Professional sport|professional]] rugby league teams. In both sides first year in co—existence, St Helens finished yet again in a disappointing low mid—table finish.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1913%2014|title=1913/1914 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1914|access-date=2 August 2010}} During the [[First World War]], St Helens struggled to compete and failed to complete the full fixture list of the Emergency War League on two occasions,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1915_16|title=1915/1916 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1916|access-date=20 July 2009}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1916_17|title=1916/1917 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1917|access-date=20 July 2009}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1917_18|title=1917/1918 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1917|access-date=20 July 2009}} with the club finishing mid—table in the first year of the war,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1914%2015|title=1914/1915 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1915|access-date=2 August 2010}} as well as being beaten by 37 points to 3 by Huddersfield in that year's Challenge Cup Final.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=1157|title=1915 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 May 1915|access-date=2 August 2010}} The aftermath of the war was still taking its toll on national sport, not merely the club's ability to compete and complete fixtures, on 31 January 1918 'close down' due to a lack of finances following a 22–0 defeat by Widnes. Saints re-open on 25 December 1918 and are beaten 20 points to nil by St Helens Recs in a friendly fixture at City Road. In the shortened 1918–1919 season, St Helens played only nine times.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1918%2019|title=1918/1919 Northern Football Union League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1919|access-date=2 August 2010}} The club's lack of success and disappointing league finishes continued for another seven 1926|access-date=2 August 2010}} The club defeated town rivals the Recs in the [[RFL Lancashire Cup|Lancashire]] [[Rugby league county cups|County Cup]] Final by 10 points to 2 in the [[1926–27 Northern Rugby Football League season|1926–27]] season.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=1519|title=1926/1927 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints in 1930.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/ray_french/2949991.stm|title=French Lessons|publisher=BBC|date=24 April 2003|access-date=24 April 2003|archive-date=16 February 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070216171501/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/ray_french/2949991.stm|url-status=live}} They won their first ever National Championship in the [[1931–32 Northern Rugby Football League season|1931–32]] season,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1931_32|title=1931/1932 National League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1932|access-date=17 July 2009}} defeating [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]] 9–5 in the final.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=1764|title=1931/1932 National League Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1931|access-date=17 July 2009}} This was the same season that they won their second Lancashire League,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1931_32|title=1930/1931 web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1929_30|title=1929/1930 Lancashire League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1930|access-date=17 July 2009}} They lost the 1933 Lancashire Cup Final to Warrington,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=1780|title=1933 Lancashire web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1932_33|title=1932/1933 National League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1933|access-date=3 August 2010}} St Helens reached no finals or achieved any more honours during the remainder of the 1930s. Also, what appeared to be building as something of an inter—town derby between the two St Helens clubs was struck down as St Helens Recs played their last game on 29 April 1939, as, due to the [[Great Depression|economic depression]], it was not possible for the town to sustain two teams.{{cite web|url=http://pilkingtonrecs.co.uk/aboutus.aspx|title=History of Pilkington Recs RLFC|publisher=pilkingtonrecs.co.uk|date=26 July 2010|access-date=26 July 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091210075408/http://pilkingtonrecs.co.uk/aboutus.aspx|archive-date=10 December 2009}} Like during the First World War, the club could not enjoy having a full—time squad during the [[Second World War]] and struggled to compete. They did not compete in the National Championship until a 17 team Emergency War League was formed in the 1941–42 season,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1929_30|title=19241/1942 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1942|access-date=17 July 2009}} and did not win any regional honours. They finished bottom of the EWL in seasons 1942–43{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1942_43|title=1942/1943 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1943|access-date=17 July 2009}} and 1943–44{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1943_44|title=1943/1944 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1944|access-date=17 July 2009}} and next-to-bottom in 1944–45.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1944_45|title=1944/1945 Emergency War League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 April 1945|access-date=17 July 2009}} ===Post-war (1945–1979)=== The club's fortunes that had seen them be successful so rarely the decade previous did not change in the 1940s. trophyless.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1950%2051|title=1950/51 National League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 1951|access-date=3 August 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1951%2052|title=1951/52 National League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 1952|access-date=3 August 2010}} The arrival of [[Jim Sullivan (Welsh rugby league)|Jim Sullivan]] as [[Coach (sport)|head coach]] in 1952{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php|title=Saints Coaching Register-see General Pages, then Coaches and Others|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 2009|access-date=1 July 2009}} heralded a successful era for St Helens, and helped to establish the club as a respected force in British and eventually world rugby league. Under Sullivan, St Helens reached, and lost, the 1952–53 Challenge Cup final.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=2549|title=1953 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 April 1953|access-date=11 August 2010}} They didn't have long to wait for trophies as St Helens won the [[Rugby league county leagues|Lancashire Leagues]], in [[1952–53 Northern Rugby Football League season|1952–53]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1951_52|title=1952–53 Lancashire League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 2009|access-date=1 July 2009}} The 1953 Championship final against Halifax was held at Manchester City's Maine Road ground; in front of a crowd of over 50,000, Saints won their second Championship 24–14. They also won the 1953 Lancashire Cup, beating Wigan 16–8 at Swinton, the first time the two old rivals had clashed in a major final, Saints. Sullivan took Saints to their first victory in the Challenge Cup final (against [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]] in 1956),{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=381|title=1956 Challenge Cup Final Commentary|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=11 July 1956|access-date=11 July 2009}} On Saturday 24 November 1956, St Helens inflicted a touring [[Australian national rugby league team|Australia]] its biggest ever defeat with a 44–2 win.{{cite web|url=http://www.rugbyrelics.com/SW/sports/rl/sullivan.htm|title=Jim Sullivan Timeline|publisher=rugbyrelics.com|date=11 July 2009|access-date=11 July 2009|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090719070020/http://www.rugbyrelics.com/SW/sports/rl/sullivan.htm|archive-date=19 July 2009}} The following year saw Saints win the 1956-7 Lancashire League [[1956–57 Northern Rugby Football League season|1956–57]]{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1956_57|title=1956–57 Lancashire League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 2009|access-date=1 July 2009}} and they won it again in [[1958–59 Northern Rugby Football League season|1958–59]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1958_59|title=1958–59 Lancashire League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 2009|access-date=1 July 2009}} Sullivan's second championship came in 1958–59.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1958_59|title=1958–59 League Table |publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 2009|access-date=1 July 2009}} A second [[RFL Lancashire Cup|Lancashire]] [[Rugby league county cups|County Cup]] came in 1960–61,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=2876|title=1961 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 2009|access-date=1 July 2009}} [[File:St. Helens home jersey 2010.svg|thumb|120px|The now synonymous red "vee" of St Helens—still used today—was first seen in the 1961 Challenge Cup Final.]] Ex—St Helens [[Captain (sports)|captain]] and [[Rugby league positions#Prop forward|prop-forward]] [[Alan Prescott]] took over from Jim Sullivan as head coach in 1959. Perhaps the golden era of the club came in the 1960s, as well as more lately in the [[History of rugby league#After 1995|Super League era]]. With a galaxy of stars including [[Tom van Vollenhoven]], [[Alex Murphy (rugby league)|Alex Murphy]], [[Dick Huddart]], [[Cliff Watson]], [[Ray French]] and [[Vince Karalius]], the 1960s was a decade of great success for the Saints. In Prescott's first season as coach he lifted the Lancashire League in [[1959–60 Northern Rugby Football League season|the 1959–60 season]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1959_60|title=1959–60 Lancashire League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 2009|access-date=1 July 2009}} During this decade, the recognisable 'red vee' strip first appeared in 1961 for the [[Challenge Cup#List of finals|final]] against [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]. St Helens won this epic 12–6,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=2909|title=1961 Challenge Cup Final |publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 1961|access-date=1 July 2009}} and the kit has since become synonymous with the club. [[Mick Sullivan]] joined Saints for £11,000, then a record transfer fee.{{citation needed|date=September 2020}} They won the Lancashire Cup in the 1961–62 season, with a 25–9 success over [[Swinton Lions|Swinton]] (at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], [[Wigan]]) seeing yet more silverware come St Helens' way under the management of Prescott.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=2924|title=1962 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=11 November 1961|access-date=6 August 2010}} After his departure in 1962, [[Stan McCormick]] led the club to retaining the Lancashire Cup in his first year,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=2968|title=1963 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=27 October 1962|access-date=6 August 2010}} again beating Swinton; and St Helens made it a quadruplet of Lancashire Cup successes with wins against Leigh in 1964,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3010|title=1964 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 October 1963|access-date=6 August 2010}} and once more Swinton in 1965, the latter success under coach Joe Coan.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3056|title=1965 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 October 1964|access-date=6 August 2010}} St Helens won the Western Division Championship under McCormick's rule, beating Swinton 10–7.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3041|title=1964 Western Championship Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=16 May 1964|access-date=12 August 2010}} St Helens, under coach Joe Coan, lost the 1964–65 Championship final to Halifax at [[Station Road, Swinton]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3086|title=1964/65 Championship Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=22 May 1965|access-date=11 August 2010}} The 1965 [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] tourists appeared at Knowsley Road on Wednesday 15 September. Saints inflicted a 28–7 defeat on their visitors,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3094|title=Saints vs. 1965 New Zealand Tour |publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 1961|access-date=1 July 2009}} their biggest loss of the tour. A [[Rugby Football League Championship|League]]{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1965_66|title=1966 League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=11 July 2009|access-date=6 August 2010}} and [[Challenge Cup|Cup]]{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3134|title=1966 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=1 July 1966|access-date=1 July 2009}} double was achieved under Coan in the 1965–66 season, whilst they lost the Floodlit Trophy final against [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3108|title=1966 Floodlit Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=14 December 1965|access-date=6 August 2010}} St Helens were beaten by [[Wakefield Trinity]] in the 1967 Rugby Football League Championship Final at Station Road, Swinton on 10 May 1967 by 20 points to 9 in a replay,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3184|title=1967 Championship Final Replay|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=10 May 1967|access-date=15 July 2009}} after a 7–7 draw 4 days earlier.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3181|title=1967 Championship Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=6 May 1967|access-date=15 July 2009}} This would be Coan's last year in charge at St Helens after a highly successful period as boss. He was replaced by [[Cliff Evans (rugby league)|Cliff Evans]]. Evans' first full season in charge at the club saw him win the club's eighth Lancashire Cup in 1968; winning 13–10 against Warrington after a replay.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3203|title=1968 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=2 December 1968|access-date=7 August 2010}} St Helens retained the Lancashire Cup the year later,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3247|title=1969 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1968|access-date=7 August 2010}} whilst also winning the Lancashire League for being the highest placed Lancashire side in the National standings,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1968_69|title=1968/69 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1969|access-date=7 August 2010}} and they also reached the final of the Floodlit Trophy that season, where they were beaten 7–4 by Wigan.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3255|title=1969 Floodlit Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=17 December 1969|access-date=7 August 2010}} The 1969–70 season would be the year that Evans would leave his post, but not without winning a National Championship, beating Leeds in the final after finishing third overall.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3328|title=1970 Championship Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=16 May 1970|access-date=7 August 2010}} The 1970s were also seen as a successful spell for St Helens, as they reached three Challenge Cup finals in the period. [[Jim Challinor]] took over from Cliff Evans in 1970, and in his first season, he won the Championship,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1970_71|title=1970/71 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1971|access-date=7 August 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3380|title=1971 Championship Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=16 May 1971|access-date=7 August 2010}} and reached two finals, the Lancashire Cup and Floodlit Trophy, losing both.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3352|title=1971 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=28 November 1970|access-date=7 August 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3357|title=1971 Floodlit Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=13 December 1970|access-date=7 August 2010}} In this season, a European Championship—not dissimilar to today's [[World Club Challenge]]—was contested between St Helens and French champions [[Saint-Gaudens Bears|St Gaudens]]. Over a two—legged game, St Helens won 92–11 on aggregate.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3354|title=1970/71 European Championship Final 1st Leg|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=5 December 1970|access-date=7 August 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3381|title=1970/71 European Championship Final 2nd Leg|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=5 January 1971|access-date=7 August 2010}} In their first Challenge Cup Final of the 1970s, they defeated [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in 1972 16–13,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3437|title=1972 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 August 1972|access-date=11 July 2009}} in addition to winning their first Floodlit Trophy, after losing out in the final three times before, with an 8–2 win over Rochdale.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3408|title=1972 Floodlit Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=14 December 1971|access-date=7 August 2010}} The club reached the Championship final in that season, but were beaten.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3438|title=1972 Championship Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=20 May 1972|access-date=11 August 2010}} No success was achieved in seasons 1972–73 and 1973–74, with St Helens finishing third and second in the respective years.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1972%2073|title=1972/73 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1973|access-date=7 August 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1973%2074|title=1973/74 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1974|access-date=7 August 2010}} This could be a possible reason for Jim Challinor's replacement with [[Eric Ashton]] as head coach. In Ashton's first season in charge, St Helens won the Championship,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1974%2075|title=1974/75 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1975|access-date=7 August 2010}} and contested the inaugural [[Rugby League Premiership|Premiership]] Final, losing 26–11 to Leeds.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3577|title=1975 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=17 May 1975|access-date=7 August 2010}} They repeated their first Challenge Cup success of the 1970s three years after the first against [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]] in 1976 where they were victorious by 20–5 in the famous 'Dads Army' final.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3626|title=1976 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 August 1976|access-date=11 July 2009}} They also won the Premiership against [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3629|title=1976 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=22 May 1976|access-date=7 August 2010}} and the Floodlit Trophy against [[Dewsbury Rams|Dewsbury]] in a successful season.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3597|title=1976 Floodlit Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=16 December 1975|access-date=7 August 2010}} In the same year, St Helens lost to [[Sydney Roosters|Eastern Suburbs]] in an unofficial World Club Challenge Final by 25 points to 2.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4430|title=1976 World Club Challenge|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=29 June 1976|access-date=7 August 2010}} The club won the Premiership in 1977,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3674|title=1977 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=28 May 1977|access-date=7 August 2010}} but, on 13 December 1977, Saints lost 26–11 to [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] in the final of the [[BBC2 Floodlit Trophy]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3695|title=1977 BBC2 Floodlit Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=13 December 1977|access-date=15 July 2009}} In 1978 Leeds avenged their 1972 loss against St Helens, emerging winners by 14–12.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3723|title=1978 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 August 1978|access-date=11 July 2009}} St Helens lost the Floodlit Trophy in the 1978–79 season, going down to Widnes.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3745|title=1979 Floodlit Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=12 December 1978|access-date=7 August 2010}} The 1979–80 season was unsuccessful, with St Helens finishing mid—table.{{cite web|url= http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1979_80|title=1979/80 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1980|access-date=7 August 2010}} Eric Ashton left the club after this disappointing year. ===The 1980s, and early to mid-1990s=== Former club player and Welsh international [[Kel Coslett]] took over as coach in June 1980.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=10&num=15112|title=Kel Coslett Bio|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=15 July 2009|access-date=15 July 2009}} However, his spell as coach was not nearly as successful as his spell as a player, and St Helens won nothing whilst under Coslett's command, finishing mid-table in both seasons.{{cite web|url= http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1980%81|title=1980/81 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1981|access-date=7 August 2010}}{{cite web|url= http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1981%82|title=1981/82 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1982|access-date=7 August 2010}} He held the post for two years before handing over to [[Billy Benyon]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=10&num=14897|title=Billy Benyon Bio|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=15 July 2009|access-date=15 July 2009}} Not in-keeping with several of his predecessors, Benyon did not enjoy any success in his first season as St Helens coach, losing the Lancashire Cup final of that year to Warrington.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3899|title=1983 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=23 December 1982|access-date=8 August 2010}} Nothing was won in the 1983–84 season,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1983_84|title=1983/84 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1984|access-date=8 August 2010}} but Saints won back the Lancashire Cup, with a 26–18 win at Wigan in the 1984–85 season.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3980|title=1985 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=28 October 1984|access-date=8 August 2010}} They also won the [[Rugby League Premiership|Premiership]] in the same season with a 36–16 victory over defending champions Hull Kingston Rovers at [[Elland Road]] in [[Leeds]]. The 1984–85 season saw the arrival (for one season only) of giant Australian international centre [[Mal Meninga]] who quickly became a crowd favourite at Knowsley Road.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4010|title=1985 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=11 May 1985|access-date=15 July 2009}} In Benyon's last season as coach, 1985–86, nothing was won.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1985%2086|title=1985/86 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1986|access-date=8 August 2010}} The arrival of [[Alex Murphy (rugby league)|Alex Murphy]] as coach in 1986{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=10&num=16031|title=Alex Murphy Bio|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=15 July 2009|access-date=15 July 2009}} produced some colourful displays from a team that was widely regarded as an entertaining team to watch, but seemed to be constant runners-up. This was illustrated by the defeat by [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]] in the Challenge Cup final at Wembley, 19–18, in 1987.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4095|title=1987 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=2 May 1987|access-date=15 July 2009}} Success was achieved in 1988 when St Helens lifted their one and only [[League Cup (rugby league)|John Player Trophy]] with a 15–14 victory over Leeds in January 1988, at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], [[Wigan]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4115|title=1988 John Player Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=9 January 1988|access-date=15 July 2009}} [[Neil Holding]] with the crucial, match-winning [[Playing rugby league#Point scoring|drop-goal]]. They, however, lost to Widnes in the Premiership Final at [[Old Trafford (football ground)|Old Trafford]] at the end of the season, further showing this team's ability to get to finals and not be able to compete on the best stage.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4135|title=1988 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=15 May 1988|access-date=15 July 2009}} Nothing was achieved in the next two seasons,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1988%2089|title=1988/89 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1990|access-date=8 August 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1989%2090|title=1989/90 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1990|access-date=8 August 2010}} including a defeat in the 1989 Challenge Cup Final{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4172|title=1989 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=29 April 1989|access-date=11 August 2010}} and Murphy stood down as coach. Murphy was succeeded by [[Mike McClennan]] in February 1990. In his first season, McClennan took St Helens to the 1991 Challenge Cup Final, where they were defeated by 13 points to 8 by Wigan.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=437|title=1991 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=27 April 1991|access-date=7 July 2010}} They won the Lancashire Cup, in the 1991–92 season, beating [[Rochdale Hornets]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=429|title=1992 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=15 May 1991|access-date=15 July 2009}} They also lost the Premiership Final that season, losing to Wigan.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=398|title=1992 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=17 May 1992|access-date=8 August 2010}} In 1993 the club avenged their defeat by Wigan the season previous to win the Premiership,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=358|title=1993 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=15 May 1993|access-date=15 July 2009}} in the same season that they won the [[Rugby League Charity Shield (Great Britain)|Charity Shield]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=397|title=1993 Charity Shield|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=23 August 1992|access-date=8 August 2010}} and lost the Lancashire Cup final.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=387|title=1993 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=18 October 1992|access-date=8 August 2010}} McClennan was head coach until December 1993, when [[Eric Hughes (rugby, born 1950)|Eric Hughes]] succeeded him as head coach in 1994. Under Hughes, St Helens only reached the one, Regal Trophy Final, where they lost to Wigan in 1996 by 25 to 16.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=258|title=1996 Regal Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=13 January 1996|access-date=8 August 2010}} They finished fourth in both of Hughes' seasons in charge.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1994%2095|title=1994/95 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1995|access-date=8 August 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1995%2096|title=1995/96 League Tables|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=25 October 1996|access-date=8 August 2010}} The lack of trophies in the St Helens cabinet, after the club had suddenly become so accustomed to success, would need to change in the "new" brand [[Super League]] that formed in 1996; hence Hughes' departure in 1996 and replacement with Australian [[Shaun McRae]]. ===Summer era=== Saints became one of the most successful side of the [[History of rugby league#After 1995|summer era]]. Since the inception of [[Super League]] in 1996, they have won the title on eight occasions, and have added seven [[Challenge Cup]]s to their five previous successes. They won the [[Super League I|inaugural Super League]] by finishing top of the league before the playoff era. St Helens defeated [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford]] 8–6 in the [[1999 Super League Grand Final]], their first Grand Final, with more than 50,000 people seeing [[Chris Joynt]] lift the trophy at Old Trafford.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=124|title=1999 Super League Grand Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=9 October 1999|access-date=16 July 2009}} They also won the [[World Club Challenge]] in [[2001 World Club Challenge|2001]] and [[2007 World Club Challenge|2007]], beating [[Brisbane Broncos|Brisbane]] both times.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=89|title=2001 World Club Challenge|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 January 2001|access-date=16 July 2009}}{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/6378807.stm|title=St Helens vs. Brisbane As It Happened|publisher=BBC|date=23 February 2007|access-date=11 July 2009|first=Phil|last=Harlow}} In 2023 they then beat Penrith Panthers 13–12 in Australia to win it for a third time. ====Late 1990s==== The success of the Saints in Super League began under the leadership of [[Shaun McRae]] in 1996. During his tenure the club won one league title ([[Super League I|1996]]),{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1996|title=1996 Super League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=9 October 1999|access-date=15 July 2009}} a year in which he was named Super League's coach of the year, and enjoyed back—to—back successes over Bradford in the Challenge Cup (1996{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=247|title=1996 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=27 April 1996|access-date=15 July 2009}} and 1997{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=214|title=1997 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=3 May 1996|access-date=15 July 2009}}). St Helens lost consecutive Premiership finals to Wigan in 1996,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=227|title=1996 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=8 September 1996|access-date=12 August 2010}} and 1997.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=190|title=1997 Premiership Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=28 September 1997|access-date=12 August 2010}} 1998 proved to be a trophyless year,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=1998|title=1998 Super League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=9 October 1998|access-date=8 August 2010}} and [[Ellery Hanley]] succeeded McRae in 1999, after the Australian left for new Super League side [[Gateshead Thunder (1999)|Gateshead]]. Hanley led Saints to [[1999 Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]] success against Bradford in October of his first year in charge. Hanley was considered by many to be a polarising figure and after several acrimonious disagreements with the St Helens board of directors, he was sacked a month into the [[Super League V|following season]].{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7144151.stm|title=Hanley Appointed Doncaster Coach|publisher=BBC|date=14 December 2007|access-date=14 December 2007}} [[Ian Millward]] was appointed as Hanley's successor as head coach. ====2000s==== =====Millward's reign and a controversial sacking (2000–2005)===== [[File:St Helens 2001.jpg|thumb|170px|right|St. Helens defeated Wigan Warriors in the [[2000 Super League Grand Final]].]] Under Millward, St Helens quickly became the most exciting team in the competition, playing expansive, attacking rugby. He saw them soundly beaten in the [[2000 World Club Challenge]], losing 44–6 to [[Melbourne Storm|Melbourne]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=121|title=2000 World Club Challenge|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 January 2000|access-date=20 July 2009}} but lead them to retaining their Super League title in [[2000 Super League Grand Final|2000]] beating [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] 29–16.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/photo_galleries/972794.stm|title=Super League IV Grand Final|publisher=BBC|date=14 October 2000|access-date=16 July 2009}} They also won the [[Challenge Cup]] in 2001; 13–6 over Bradford,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=77|title=2001 Challenge Cup Final Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=28 April 2001|access-date=16 July 2009}} with the final held at [[Twickenham Stadium]] for the first time,{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/4864638.stm|title=RFL Remains Bullish on Twickenham|publisher=BBC|date=8 March 2006|access-date=16 July 2009}} and the [[2001 World Club Challenge]], earning a 20–18 win over the Broncos.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/6380851.stm|title=World Club Challenge Chris Joynt|publisher=BBC|date=22 February 2007|access-date=16 July 2009}} Millward then lead Saints to the top of [[Super League VII]], and to reclaim the Super League title in the [[2002 Super League Grand Final|2002 Grand Final]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=2002|title=Super League VII Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 September 2002|access-date=12 August 2010}} [[Sean Long]] snatching a last minute 19–18 win over the [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford]] with a [[Playing rugby league#Point scoring|drop—goal]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=17|title=2002 Super League Grand Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=19 October 2002|access-date=16 July 2009}} They lost the 2002 Challenge Cup Final to Wigan at [[Murrayfield Stadium]] by 21 points to 12.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=41|title=2002 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=27 April 2002|access-date=11 August 2010}} They were hammered in the [[2003 World Club Challenge]] by [[Sydney Roosters|Sydney]] by 38 points to 0.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=15|title=2003 World Club Challenge|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=14 February 2003|access-date=20 July 2009}} In this season, they failed to win a trophy after being knocked out of the Challenge Cup by Leeds at the semi—final stage, and the Super League play—offs by Wigan at the same stage. In 2004 they beat arch—rivals Wigan 32–16 at the [[Millennium Stadium]], [[Cardiff]] in front of a capacity crowd of 73,734 people to win the Challenge Cup,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4509|title=2004 Challenge Cup Final Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=15 May 2004|access-date=16 July 2009}} Long gaining his second of an eventual three [[Lance Todd Trophy|Lance Todd Trophies]].{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/4169864.stm|title=Lance Todd Trophy Winners|publisher=BBC|date=26 August 2006|access-date=16 July 2009}} Millward's reign was not without controversy and his St Helens career ended controversially after he was suspended in May 2005.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/st_helens/4515515.stm|title=Millward Suspended For Swearing|publisher=BBC|date=6 May 2005|access-date=16 July 2009}} He was sacked for gross misconduct a week later.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/st_helens/4527831.stm|title=Boss Millward Sacked By St Helens|publisher=BBC|date=10 May 2005|access-date=16 July 2009}} Daniel Anderson was appointed as coach, Millward was then made coach of archrivals Wigan.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/4570497.stm|title=Millward Appointed Coach of Wigan|publisher=BBC|date=22 May 2005|access-date=16 July 2009}} =====The Anderson era (2005–2008)===== [[File:St Helens 2006 CCF.jpg|thumb|St Helens lifting the Challenge Cup trophy after the [[2006 Challenge Cup|2006]] Final]] Daniel Anderson saw his new team become the first team to top the [[Super League]] and fail to reach [[Old Trafford (football ground)|Old Trafford]] in 2005.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=2005|title=2005 Super League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=9 October 2005|access-date=16 July 2009}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4568|title=2005 Super League Play-offs Final Eliminator|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=7 October 2005|access-date=16 July 2009}} However, St Helens won the [[2006 Challenge Cup]] Final at [[Twickenham Stadium]], beating [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]] 42–12.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/5271814.stm|title=St Helens 42–12 Huddersfield |publisher=BBC|date=26 August 2006|access-date=11 July 2009}} [[Rugby league positions#Scrum half|Scrum-half]] Sean Long was awarded the [[Lance Todd trophy]] for a record third time for his man—of—the—match performance during the final. St Helens followed up their Challenge Cup win by claiming the [[League Leader's Shield]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=2006|title=2006 Super League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=9 October 2006|access-date=16 July 2009}} before cementing their reputation as the team of the year by defeating Hull 26 points to 4 in the [[2006 Super League Grand Final|Super League Grand Final]].{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/6046328.stm|title=St Helens 26–4 Hull FC|publisher=BBC|date=6 October 2006|access-date=8 July 2008}} Once more St Helens confirmed their status as the outstanding team of the summer era. Additionally, [[Paul Wellens]] received the [[Man of Steel Award]] for the 2006 season.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/4169864.stm|title=Wellens Wins Man of Steel Award|publisher=BBC|date=9 August 2006|access-date=16 July 2009}} In December 2006 St Helens were awarded with the [[BBC Sports Personality of the Year Team Award]] at the Annual [[BBC Sports Personality of the Year]] Ceremony. This accolade recognises the best team in any sport within the United Kingdom. At the same ceremony [[Daniel Anderson (rugby league)|Daniel Anderson]] was given the [[BBC Sports Personality of the Year Coach Award]] – this was the first time a rugby league coach had won the award.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/front_page/6220542.stm|title=Sports Personality Winners 2006|publisher=BBC|date=10 December 2006|access-date=11 July 2009}} After a slow start to the [[Super League XII|2007 season]], Saints added to their history by beating [[Brisbane Broncos|Brisbane]] 18–14 to win the [[2007 World Club Challenge]]. {| class="toccolours" ! {{fontcolor|Red|St Helens 2007 World Club Championship winning team}} |- |align=centre|
{| |----- |'''8''' Nick Fozzard (PR) | |'''14''' James Roby (HK) | |'''10''' Jason Cayless (PR) |----- | |'''11''' Lee Gilmour (SR) | |'''15''' Mike Bennett (SR) |----- | | |'''12''' Jon Wilkin (LF) | || |----- | colspan=7 | |----- || |'''7''' Sean Long (SH) |----- | || || |'''6''' Leon Pryce (SO) |----- | || || || |'''4''' Willie Talau (CE) |----- | || || || || |'''3''' Matt Gidley (CE) |----- |'''5''' Francis Meli (WG) | || || || || |'''2''' Ade Gardner (WG) |----- | || || |'''1''' Paul Wellens (FB) |----- |Substitutes |- |'''9''' Keiron Cunningham (HK) |- |'''18''' Bryn Hargreaves (PR) |- |'''13''' Paul Sculthorpe (LF) |- |'''17''' James Graham (PR) |}
|} In July, they beat Super League rivals, [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4633|title=2007 Challenge Cup Semi-Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=28 July 2007|access-date=16 July 2009}} to reach the first Challenge Cup final at the new Wembley Stadium.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/6503783.stm|title=Challenge Cup Returns To Wembley|publisher=BBC|date=28 March 2007|access-date=16 July 2009}} Here, St Helens successfully defended their Challenge Cup by defeating [[Catalans Dragons]] 30–8 in the [[2007 Challenge Cup Final|final]] on 25 August 2007.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/6962139.stm|title=St Helens vs. Catalans As It Happened FC|publisher=BBC|date=25 August 2008|access-date=11 July 2009|first=Rob|last=Hodgetts}} They were beaten in the [[2007 Super League Grand Final|Grand Final that year]] by [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] by 33 points to 6,{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7043251.stm|title=St Helens 6–33 Leeds Rhinos|publisher=BBC|date=13 October 2007|access-date=8 July 2008|first=Elizabeth|last=Hudson}} despite finishing at the top of the [[Super League XII#Table|league ladder]] for the third successive season.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=2007|title=2007 Super League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=9 October 2007|access-date=16 July 2009}} [[James Roby]], however, became the second St Helens player, and home—grown talent in two years to win the Man of Steel Award. Saints success in the Challenge Cup continued in [[2008 Challenge Cup Final|2008]] with a victory at the new [[Wembley Stadium]], this time defeating [[Hull F.C.]] 28–16.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7585584.stm|title=St Helens 28–16 Hull FC|publisher=BBC|date=30 August 2008|access-date=8 July 2008|first=Pranav|last=Soneji}} Paul Wellens received the [[Lance Todd Trophy]] after sharing the award with team-mate [[Leon Pryce]] the year earlier. They also achieved first place again in the [[Super League XIII|2008 Super League]] season—for the fourth year running{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=24&tabledate=2008|title=2008 Super League Table|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=9 October 2008|access-date=16 July 2009}}—winning the [[League Leaders Shield]], and beat Leeds 38–10 for the right to go to Old Trafford to contest the Grand Final.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4676|title=2008 Super League Play-offs Qualifying Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=4 October 2008|access-date=8 July 2008}} However, St Helens were once again defeated by Leeds in the [[2008 Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]], by a margin of 24 to 16, on 4 October 2008.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7652600.stm|title=St Helens 16–24 Leeds Rhinos|publisher=BBC|date=4 October 2008|access-date=8 July 2008|first=Sam|last=Lyon}} [[James Graham (rugby league)|James Graham]], on a positive note, made it a hat—trick of ex—Blackbrook Royals to win the Man of Steel Award whilst playing for St Helens. This would be Anderson's last game in charge of the club, as he announced he was to return to Australia and the [[Parramatta Eels]] of the [[National Rugby League|NRL]]. St Helens fans and players alike were saddened to see Anderson leave, after not only upholding the tradition of St Helens exciting brand of rugby, but giving them a defensive and disciplined edge that was never apparent under Ian Millward. His personality and relationship with the fans was an additional reason why St Helens fans were disgruntled in him leaving after four years in charge and why next boss [[Michael Potter|Mick Potter]] faced a fair amount of criticism in his initial period as boss. =====The Potter years (2009–2010)===== [[File:Widnes Vikings vs St Helens Karalius Cup 2010 002.JPG|thumb|right|200px|St Helens against [[Widnes Vikings]] in the pre-season 2010 Karalius Cup]] Mick Potter was announced as the successor to Anderson, which received many plaudits from the St. Helens fans and the European game as a whole, as the year previous he had led the [[Catalans Dragons]] to a record—high third—placed finish in the league. On 9 August 2009, St Helens reached a record ninth consecutive [[Challenge Cup]] semi—final,{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12214_5359870,00.html|title=Cup Venues Announced|publisher=skysports.com|date=2 June 2009|access-date=10 August 2009}} where they were beaten by 24 points to 14 by the [[Huddersfield Giants]].{{cite web|url=http://saintsrlfc.com/results/120|title=St Helens vs. Huddersfield CC match report|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=9 August 2009|access-date=10 August 2009}} This prevented Saints from reaching the final at the [[Wembley Stadium|new Wembley Stadium]] for a third successive time and from winning the cup for a fourth time running. This defeat naturally came as a shock to the St Helens faithful, after so often in the last 15 years seeing the team reach the pinnacle of this competition and go on to win the cup. It was from here that the criticism began, and questions were raised particularly of his tactics and his activity (or lack of) in the transfer market. On 3 October 2009, Saints defeated fierce rivals the Wigan Warriors to book their place at a fourth consecutive [[Super League Grand Final]],{{cite web|url=http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3202308|title=St Helens vs. Wigan P-O match report|publisher=engagesuperleague.co.uk|date=3 October 2009|access-date=3 October 2009}} only to lose out to the Leeds Rhinos in the final, 18–10, making the Leeds Rhinos the only team to win the Grand Final three times consecutively. A 20-year-old [[Kyle Eastmond]] scored all of Saints' points.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8290503.stm|title=St Helens 10–18 Leeds Rhinos|publisher=BBC|date=10 October 2009|access-date=21 October 2009}} A trophyless year for the first time since 2003 was another catalyst to Potter's critics abusing and slating his appointment, with even rumours of rifts within the changing rooms. ====2010s==== [[File:James Roby Saints.JPG|thumb|right|200px|[[James Roby]] in 2010]] 2010 was the year that saw Potter surrender to his critics and leave St. Helens. Criticisms from club legends like Paul Sculthorpe and Sean Long regarding his personal skills with the fans,{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6148818,00.html|title=Long – Potter exit no shock|publisher=skysports.com|date=12 May 2010|access-date=12 July 2010}} as well as the continued fan rejection saw him let his contract run out and, initially, seek a job in the NRL,{{cite web|url=http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=17560|title=Change of scenery for Potter|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=4 May 2010|access-date=15 June 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120120034707/http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=17560|archive-date=20 January 2012}}{{cite web|url=http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=17573|title=Gidley empathises with Potter|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=5 May 2010|access-date=15 June 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100507005947/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=17573|archive-date=7 May 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=17654|title=Long not surprised by Potter exit|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=12 May 2010|access-date=15 June 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120118171313/http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=17654|archive-date=18 January 2012}} but eventually, and perhaps surprisingly, join Bradford on a two—year contract.{{cite web|url=http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=18413|title=Potter lands Bradford Bulls job|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=16 July 2010|access-date=16 July 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120120132833/http://superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=18413|archive-date=20 January 2012}} Names like [[Royce Simmons]],{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/jun/28/rugby-league-st-helens-coach|title=Royce Simmons the surprising front-runner to take over at St Helens|work=guardian|date=28 June 2010|access-date=12 July 2010 | location=London | first=Andy | last=Wilson}} [[Mal Meninga]],{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6184167,00.html|title=Meninga 'would consider' Saints|publisher=skysports.com|date=1 June 2010|access-date=12 July 2010}} and assistant coach [[Kieron Purtill]],{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6239158,00.html|title=Purtill waits on Saints job|publisher=skysports.com|date=30 June 2010|access-date=12 July 2010}} were linked with the job for 2011. Simmons was the chosen man for the job, as announced on 22 July 2010.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2698|title=Saints Appoint Royce Simmons|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=22 July 2010|access-date=22 July 2010}}{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6274263,00.html|title=Simmons to coach Saints|publisher=skysports.com|date=22 July 2010|access-date=22 July 2010}} The 2010 season would also see legendary hooker and captain Keiron Cunningham retire from the game after 17 years with his one and only club. However, Cunningham would not be leaving without leaving a lasting legacy on the club. A lifelike bronze statue of Cunningham was built and placed on display in the town, before being transported to the club's new stadium upon its construction in 2012.{{cite web|url=http://sthelensnews.blogspot.com/2007/12/kieron-cunningham-is-voted-man-of.html|title=Cunningham is Man of Bronze|publisher=sthelensnews.com|date=4 June 2008|access-date=8 July 2008}}{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2009/feb/08/keiron-cunningham-st-helens-rugby-super-league|title=Cunningham Finds Permanent Place With His Saints|publisher=The Guardian Newspaper|date=8 February 2008|access-date=8 July 2008 | location=London | first=David | last=Lawrenson}} He would additionally take up a coaching role with the academy, as well as a strength and conditioning role with the first team. In light of these decisions, neither Cunningham nor Potter halted their personal and the St Helens team quest for success; shown by their tenth successive semi—final appearance in the [[2010 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2573|title=Potter: Semi-final Continues Saints Success|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=15 June 2010|access-date=15 June 2010}} However, Saints failure to perform on the big stage was once more shown, going down in this semi—final by 32–28.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2728|title=Saints' Final Hopes Dashed By Rhinos|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=7 August 2010|access-date=7 August 2010}} Defeating Huddersfield Giants in the qualifying semi—final in the [[2010 Super League season results#Play-offs|2010 play-off series]] by 42–22 not only saw Saints qualify for their fifth Grand Final in five years, but also saw the final ever game to be played at Knowsley Road. Fittingly, Cunningham snatched the final ever try at the famous old ground in typical fashion from dummy—half.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/168|title=St Helens 42–22 Huddersfield Giants|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=24 September 2010|access-date=5 October 2010}} However, for the fourth year running, St Helens once more proved flops in the [[2010 Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]]. One of the finest finals of the Super League era was anticipated,{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12198_6418143,00.html|title=Too close to call|publisher=Sky Sports|date=1 October 2010|access-date=5 October 2010}} but the flamboyant Saints that the fans saw so rarely under Potter once more failed to materialise, and, in front of a near sell—out crowd of 71,526, they fell to a 22–10 loss to rivals Wigan.{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3326090,00.html|title=Warriors too good for Saints|publisher=Sky Sports|date=2 October 2010|access-date=5 October 2010}} It was not the romantic finish to the Saints careers of Potter, Cunningham or any of the departing members of the squad that many had hoped for, but nevertheless, a new era was just around the corner, as Simmons' reign began. =====Beginning of a new era (2011–2018)===== {{Update section|date=August 2017}} Australian [[Royce Simmons]] took over from compatriot Mick Potter for the start of the 2011 season. As the club awaited completion of the new stadium, all home fixtures in 2011 were played at the [[Halton Stadium]] in [[Widnes]], effectively meaning St Helens were forced to play an entire season of away fixtures. In addition, they suffered upheaval in terms of the playing squad; having already lost the influential Cunningham to retirement and other club legends like Sean Long, the start of the season was overshadowed by the news that [[Kyle Eastmond]], who had been earmarked as Long's replacement, and inspirational leader [[James Graham (rugby league)|James Graham]] were both looking to leave the club. A number of serious injuries to further key players such as [[Leon Pryce]] and [[Ade Gardner]] meant the team was facing an uphill battle on the field all season. However, despite all the problems faced, St Helens defied the odds to reach their 11th Challenge Cup semi-final in a row, and at the end of the season they qualified for their sixth consecutive Grand Final. However, for the fifth year in a row they were on the losing side, as the injury-plagued squad finally succumbed to Leeds. However, the 2011 season saw the emergence of a new crop of talent, with players such as [[Jonny Lomax]] and [[Lee Gaskell]] stepping up to fill the gaps left by senior players and earning rave reviews for their performances.{{According to whom|date=April 2017}} In 2012, the club moved into their new home at [[Langtree Park]]. However, the season started with a terrible run of results, which culminated in Royce Simmons being sacked in March. With assistant coach [[Kieron Purtill]] also leaving, the coaching [[Rein#In popular expression|reins]] were taken up by youth coach Mike Rush for the remainder of the 2012 season, with Keiron Cunningham acting as his assistant. Following St Helens's defeat by Wigan in the quarter-final of the Challenge Cup, which ended a run of 11 consecutive semi-final appearances, it was announced that [[Nathan Brown (rugby league born 1973)|Nathan Brown]] would be taking over as head coach for 2013, with Rush moving back into his previous role. At the end of the [[Super League XIX]] regular season, St Helens claimed the [[League Leaders' Shield]] and reached the [[2014 Super League Grand Final]] against Wigan where they won the match 14–6 claiming their 13th premiership.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/oct/11/st-helens-wigan-super-league-grand-final-match-report/|title=St Helens win Grand Final after Wigan's Ben Flower is sent off|website=The Guardian|date=11 October 2014}} In the [[Super League XXIII]] season, St Helens claimed the [[League Leader's Shield]] and [[Ben Barba]] who had joined the club that year won the [[Man of Steel Awards|Man of Steel]] award. St Helens were favourites to reach another grand final but were upset in the preliminary final by Warrington 18–13 at Langtree Park.{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/45721728|title=Super League semi-final: Warrington beat St Helens 18–13 to reach Grand Final|work=BBC Sport|date=4 October 2018}} During the [[Super League XXIV]] season, St Helens reached the [[Challenge Cup]] final against Warrington but lost the match 18–4 at [[Wembley Stadium]].{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/49436134|title=Warrington Wolves beat St Helens 18–4|work=BBC Sport|date=24 August 2019}} ===== The "Four-Peat" and World Club Challenge ===== At the end of the [[Super League XXIV]] regular season, St Helens won the [[League Leader's Shield]] for a second consecutive year after finished 16 points clear of second placed [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]].{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/11777123/st-helens-win-2019-super-league-league-leaders-shield/|title=St Helens win 2019 Super League League Leaders' shield|website=Sky Sports}} St Helens would go on to reach the [[2019 Super League Grand Final]] at [[Old Trafford]] against [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]]. St Helens won the match 23–6 securing their 14th championship.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2019/oct/12/st-helens-v-salford-red-devils-super-league-grand-final-live/|title=St Helens 23–6 Salford Red Devils: Super League Grand Final – as it happened|website=The Guardian|date=12 October 2019|last1=Unwin|first1=Will}} At the end of the 2020 season, St Helens finished second behind league leaders Wigan. After easily accounting for [[Catalans Dragons]] in the semi-final, St Helens played against Wigan in the [[2020 Super League Grand Final]]. With only seconds remaining and with the scores locked at 4–4, St Helens scored a try after the siren through Jack Welsby to win back to back championships in the most dramatic of circumstances.{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/55072997|title=Super League Grand Final: Wigan 4–8 St Helens|work=BBC Sport }} On 17 July 2021, St. Helens won the Challenge Cup for the first time in 13 years beating Castleford in the final 26–12. St. Helens had trailed the match at half-time 12–6.{{cite web|url=https://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/st-helens-celebrate-challenge-cup-21081897/|title=How St Helens players have celebrated their first Challenge Cup win in 13 years|website=Manchestereveningnews.co.uk|access-date=18 July 2021|archive-date=18 July 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210718102540/https://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/st-helens-celebrate-challenge-cup-21081897|url-status=dead}} St Helens finished the [[2021 Super League season]] in second place on the table.{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12204/12415081/barrie-mcdermotts-2021-betfred-super-league-season-review/|title=Barrie McDermott's 2021 Betfred Super League season review|website=Skysports.com}} They then went on to defeat [[Catalan Dragons]] 12–10 in a hotly-contested Grand Final, securing the club's first league and cup double since 2006 and an historic 'three-peat' – becoming only the second team in the summer era to win three consecutive championships.{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/58858850|title=Super League Grand Final: Catalans Dragons 10–12 St Helens: Saints win three in a row|work=BBC Sport }} St Helens ended the [[Super League XXVII|2022]] regular season as League Leaders, thus earning them a bye to the semi-finals. They beat Salford 19–12, to reach their 4th consecutive Grand Final. They faced [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in the [[2022 Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]], after Leeds beat Catalans in the eliminators, and Wigan in the semi-final. St Helens won the match 24–12, to win their fourth final in a row, and by doing so, they become the first team in Super League history to win four in a row.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2022/sep/24/st-helens-leeds-super-league-grand-final-match-report-rugby-league/|title=St Helens sink Leeds to win record fourth consecutive Grand Final|newspaper=The Observer|date=24 September 2022 |last1=Bower |first1=Aaron }} Before the start of the 2023 Super League season, St Helens travelled to Australia for the [[2023 World Club Challenge]]. A week before the match, St Helens played one pre-season trial fixture against [[St. George Illawarra]] which St Helens won 30–18 at [[WIN Stadium]].{{cite web|url=https://www.rugby-league.com/article/61402/st-helens-win-battle-of-the-red-v-against-st-george-illawarra-dragons/|title=St Helens win 'Battle of the Red V' against St George Illawarra Dragons|website=Rugby-league.com}} In the week leading up to the World Club Challenge game against [[Penrith Panthers|Penrith]], many pundits, former players and coaches predicted St Helens would lose the match heavily with [[Phil Gould (rugby league)|Phil Gould]] stating "Penrith should be allowed to declare at half-time". St Helens would go on to win the World Club Challenge game 13–12 in golden point extra-time to claim their third trophy in the competition. St Helens had led the match 12–0 until a late try by Penrith took the game into extra-time.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2023/feb/18/st-helens-defeat-penrith-panthers-to-win-world-club-challenge#:~:text=Phil%20%E2%80%9CGus%E2%80%9D%20Gould%2C%20the,s%5D%20to%20the%20punch%20early./|title=Penrith Panthers lick their wounds after World Club Challenge loss to St Helens|newspaper=The Guardian|date=18 February 2023 }} {| class="toccolours" ! {{fontcolor|Red|St Helens 2023 World Club Championship winning team}} |- |align=centre|
