|} ===== Post 2023 World Club Challenge ===== In the [[2023 Super League season]], St Helens finished third on the table equal on points with league leaders Wigan and the Catalans Dragons. In the elimination playoff, the club defeated Warrington which set up a match with Catalans at the Stade Gilbert Brutus. St Helens lead the match until the final minute when Catalans player Sam Tomkins scored a try underneath the posts to win the match for Catalans and end St Helens four-year dominance of the competition.{{cite web|url=https://www.sthelensstar.co.uk/sport/23856044.paul-wellens-q-saints-review-year-look-2024/|title=Paul Wellens Q&A: Saints review of year and a look to 2024|website=Sthelensstar.co.uk|date=14 October 2023 |access-date=17 October 2023}} St Helens began the [[2024 Super League season]] strongly winning six of their opening seven games. However, injuries in the middle of the season to key players saw results drop. On 26 July 2024, St Helens were defeated by Leigh 46–4 which meant the club had lost five matches in a row for the first time since the Super League era began.{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/crgmp28gg0do|title=Eight-try Leigh pile misery on out-of-form Saints|website=Bbc.co.uk|access-date=30 July 2024}} St Helens would finish the 2024 Super League season in sixth place on the table, their lowest finish of the Super League era. The club were eliminated in the first week of the [[2024 Super League season#Week 1: Eliminators|playoffs against Warrington]]. St Helens were not expected to win the game but took Warrington to golden point extra-time where [[George Williams (rugby league, born 1994)|George Williams]] would kick the winning drop goal for Warrington.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2024/sep/28/warrington-st-helens-super-league-eliminator-playoff-rugby-league-match-report/|title= ‘Soul destroying’: Warrington eliminate St Helens in golden drop goal thriller|website=Theguardian.com}} In the [[2025 Super League season]], St Helens would finish 5th on the table. In the playoff eliminator against Leeds, the club trailed by 10 points with less than nine minutes remaining before they scored two tries to win the match 16–14. The final try was scored after the full-time siren and was reminiscent of the "wide to west" try which was scored by the club 25 years prior. In the semi-final against league leaders Hull Kingston Rovers, the club would succumb to a 20–12 loss which ended their season.{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/ce86r25lrxno/|title=Saints snatch stunning play-off victory at Leeds|website=Bbc.co.uk|access-date=7 October 2025}} ==Academy== Players who began their professional career in the academy and reserves (previously known as the "St Helens Colts") include [[Steve Prescott]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=10&num=16189|title=Steve Prescott profile|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=3 November 2010|access-date=3 November 2010}} [[Paul Forber]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=10&num=15329|title=Paul Forber profile|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=3 November 2010|access-date=3 November 2010}} [[Gary Connolly]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=10&num=15098|title=Gary Connolly profile|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=3 November 2010|access-date=3 November 2010}} and [[Chris Arkwright]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=10&num=14805&num=14805|title=Chris Arkwright profile|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=3 November 2010|access-date=3 November 2010}} Players have come from areas such as Widnes, Wigan, Cumbria and Oldham. Daniel Brotherton, a winger from [[Northampton]], signed a professional contract with the club from Northampton Demons.{{cite web|url=http://www.northamptonrl.co.uk/dan-brotherton-signs-for-saints-rl-that-is|title=Dan Brotherton signs for Saints – RL that is!|publisher=northamptonrl.co.uk|date=25 August 2010|access-date=3 November 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2775|title=Saints Sign Daniel Brotherton|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=23 August 2010|access-date=3 November 2010}} ==Women's side== {{main|St Helens R.F.C. Women}} ==Rivalries== ===Wigan Warriors=== [[File:St Helens vs Wigan 2009.JPG|thumb|right|250px|St Helens against Wigan in the semi-final play-off eliminator in 2009]] {{main|St Helens R.F.C.–Wigan Warriors rivalry}} There is a strong [[Derbies in the Rugby Football League|rivalry]] between St Helens and [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]; the [[local derby]] between the two clubs has been traditionally regarded as the biggest in British [[rugby league]], as well as one of the oldest in world rugby league.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=481|title=1st St Helens vs. Wigan game|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=16 November 1895|access-date=12 July 2009}} Both being founder members of the [[Rugby Football League|Northern Rugby Football Union]], the derby has been played since 1895, making it amongst the most historical derbys in both British and global rugby league. Remarkably, the first encounter between the fierce rivals ended in a 0–0 draw at [[Knowsley Road]], The games were traditionally played on [[Boxing Day]], however were moved to [[Good Friday]], during the busy Easter period in rugby league. More recently, the game was even played on [[Maundy Thursday]] in Super League. In all league encounters between the two since 1895, there have been 235 games, with Wigan having won nearly double the number of games that St Helens have. Saints winning 83, drawing 11 and losing 141.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/matchcentre/1541|title=Saints vs. Wigan Head-to-Head Since 1895|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=29 August 1895|access-date=2 August 2009}} All competitive games, i.e. cup and league games combined, show that in the 366 games played, St Helens have won 141, there have been 19 draws and Wigan have won 206. They too have contested no fewer than 6 [[Challenge Cup]] Finals, and have met in three [[Super League Grand Final]]s; St Helens winning 29–16 in [[2000 Super League Grand Final|2000]], with Wigan initially gaining revenge in [[2010 Super League Grand Final|2010]], with a 22–10 win, before Saints claimed the 2014 Super League Grand Final by a score of 14–6, taking a 2–1 lead in their Super League Grand Final head-to-heads. Wigan and St Helens have also met in 4 [[Rugby League Premiership|Premiership]] Finals, Wigan winning 3, St Helens once, 3 [[RFL Lancashire Cup|Lancashire]] [[Rugby league county cups|County Cup]] Finals, St Helens winning two, Wigan one,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=2566|title=1953 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=24 October 1953|access-date=31 July 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3980|title=1984 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=28 October 1984|access-date=31 July 2010}} and one [[BBC2 Floodlit Trophy|Floodlit Trophy]] Final in 1968, Wigan winning 7–4.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3255|title=1968 Floodlit Trophy Final|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=17 December 1968|access-date=31 July 2010}} The two have traditionally met each other in the [[Magic Weekend]] too. The two teams have a pretty even record, winning two (at [[Millennium Stadium]] in 2007 and 2008) and losing two (at [[Murrayfield Stadium|Murrayfield]] in 2009 and [[City of Manchester Stadium|Etihad Stadium]] in 2012), with one draw (at Millennium Stadium in 2011) in five ties.{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=1245|title=2007 Magic Weekend|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=5 May 2007|access-date=20 February 2011}}{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=2538|title=2008 Magic Weekend|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=4 May 2008|access-date=20 February 2011}}{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=4084|title=2009 Magic Weekend|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=2 May 2009|access-date=20 February 2011}}{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=21003|title=2011 Magic Weekend|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=12 February 2011|access-date=20 February 2011|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120905094216/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=21003|archive-date=5 September 2012}} St Helens greatest winning margin and the highest game score between the two in competitive football was a 75–0 win in a 2005 Challenge Cup round 6 game.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=4555|title=St Helens 75–0 Wigan|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 June 2005|access-date=31 July 2010}} Wigan's biggest win was a 65–12 win in [[Super League II]], 1997.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=210|title=St Helens 12–65 Wigan|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=26 May 1997|access-date=31 July 2010}} {| class="wikitable" width="20%" style="font-size:90%" style="text-align: center" |- ! colspan=11 | Super League record |- ! Win !! Draw !! Loss |- |26 || 4 || 30 |}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=17|title=Super League record vs Wigan|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=20 February 2011|access-date=20 February 2011}} ===Bradford Bulls=== Saints contested several finals in the modern game with [[Bradford Bulls]], following up from their vast successes respectively in previous decades. When known as Bradford Northern, Bradford experienced their period of success largely in the 1940s, at a time when St Helens struggled to compete due to the commitments of World War II. In fact, it was only in the 1950s that St Helens won their first Challenge Cup and National League, and at this time, when St Helens were establishing themselves and improving in the 1950s, and 1960s, Bradford were disproving, and in fact folded in 1963. So the contest between the two can be doubted as a historical or traditional one. However, during the modern, Summer era, the game between the two gained prestige. The two contested consecutive Challenge Cup finals in 1996 and 1997,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=247|title=St Helens 40 v 32 Bradford Bulls|publisher=saints.org.uk|date=27 April 1996|access-date=24 June 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=214|title=St Helens 32 v 22 Bradford Bulls|publisher=saints.org.uk|date=3 May 1997|access-date=24 June 2010}} and later in 2001,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=77|title=St Helens 13 v 6 Bradford Bulls|publisher=saints.org.uk|date=28 April 2001|access-date=24 June 2010}} St Helens winning all three. In Super League Grand Finals, St Helens and Bradford met twice, in [[1999 Super League Grand Final|1999]] and [[2002 Super League Grand Final|2002]], St Helens again winning all encounters.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=124|title=St Helens 8 v 6 Bradford Bulls|publisher=saints.org.uk|date=9 October 1999|access-date=24 June 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=17|title=St Helens 19 v 18 Bradford Bulls|publisher=saints.org.uk|date=19 October 2002|access-date=24 June 2010}} {| class="wikitable" width="20%" style="font-size:90%" style="text-align: center" |- ! colspan=11 | Super League record |- ! Win !! Draw !! Loss |- |24 || 1 || 20 |}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=17|title=Super League record vs Bradford|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=20 February 2011|access-date=20 February 2011}} ===Leeds Rhinos=== [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] have arguably the strongest rivalry with St Helens, out of all the [[Yorkshire]] clubs that have a history with St Helens. They have contested the last three Super League finals, but the rugby they have produced in recent years is considered amongst the best in Super League. The derby is also sometimes considered a contest in terms of pride between the two counties. Leeds and St Helens have also a historical background, contesting the 1971–72 and 1977–78 Challenge Cup Finals, each team winning one each.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3437|title=St Helens 16–13 Leeds|publisher=saints.org.uk|date=13 May 1972|access-date=24 June 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3723|title=St Helens 12–14 Leeds|publisher=saints.org.uk|date=13 May 1978|access-date=24 June 2010}} However, the derby is often noted for its bad behaviour—on and off the field. Especially recently, with incidents like the [[Ryan Bailey (rugby league)|Ryan Bailey]] "chicken wing" tackle on [[Maurie Fa'asavalu]] in 2008, the presence of [[James Graham (rugby league)|James Graham]] when the two meet, as well as the numerous fights that have broken out between the two sets of players, the game is certainly regarded as a feisty one, as well as one that produces entertaining rugby. Such activities off the field and between games like fights between supporters, has led to some fans being discouraged from attending the fixture at Knowsley Road; shown by somewhat disappointing crowd figures, such as an example of 11,048 in 2010.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/results/150|title=St Helens 24–28 Leeds|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=3 July 2010|access-date=26 July 2010}} {| class="wikitable" width="20%" style="font-size:90%" style="text-align: center" |- ! colspan=11 | Super League record |- ! Win !! Draw !! Loss |- |24 || 0 || 23 |}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=17|title=Super League record vs Leeds|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=20 February 2011|access-date=20 February 2011}} ===Warrington Wolves=== The other "big" [[North West England|North West]] club in Super League, [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]], have also built up an anticipated derby contest with St Helens, particularly within Super League. As St Helens are unbeaten at Knowsley Road against the Wire since 1996, as well as boasting a generally impressive record against the Wolves in all meetings in Super League, the game is seen as an opportunity for Warrington to rectify their record against the Saints. In terms of cup and league final meetings; the two have met in two Lancashire Cup finals, St Helens winning once after a replay in 1967,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3203|title=St Helens 13–10 Warrington|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=2 February 1967|access-date=31 July 2010}} and one Premiership final, St Helens winning in 1977.{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=7&seq=3674|title=St Helens 32–20 Warrington|publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=28 May 1977|access-date=31 July 2010}} However, they remarkably have never met in Challenge Cup or Super League Finals. On 26 February 2011, Warrington Wolves beat St. Helens for the first time in 10 years ending the Saints Hoodoo over the club. The fixture was played at the Saints temporary home at Widnes. The Wolves also beat the Saints in the reverse fixture to do the league double for the first time in 17 years. In 2012 the Wolves beat St. Helens in the Grand Final elimination clash at Langtree Park to book the Warrington Wolves a place at the Grand Final.{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/19718693|title=St Helens 18–36 Warrington|work=BBC Sport |date=29 September 2012|access-date=24 April 2017}} {| class="wikitable" width="20%" style="font-size:90%" style="text-align: center" |- ! colspan=11 | Super League record |- ! Win !! Draw !! Loss |- |35 || 2 || 9 |}{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=17|title=Super League record vs Warrington |publisher=Saints Heritage Society|date=20 February 2011|access-date=20 February 2011}} ==Associates== ===Junior rugby in St Helens=== There is a massive{{Clarify|reason=vague|date=April 2017}} junior and youth contingent of rugby league players in St Helens. Clubs such as [[Thatto Heath Crusaders|Thatto Heath]] and [[Blackbrook A.R.L.F.C.|Blackbrook Royals]] have produced many of the former and current superstars in St Helens' squads over the years, and continue to do so.{{cite web|url=http://saintsrlfc.com/team/1|title=St Helens 1st team squad|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=11 December 2009|access-date=11 December 2009}} Clubs in the St Helens area also include Bold Miners, [[Portico Vine ARLFC|Portico Vine]], [[Pilkington Recs]], Haydock Warriors, Newton Storm, Eccleston Lions and Haresfinch Hawks which provide players for St Helens through the junior years and the scholarship schemes at the club, before eventually signing professionally at 16. But St Helens' youth roots do not stop in the St Helens area. Indeed, club legend [[Keiron Cunningham]] signed for the club from Wigan St Judes,{{cite web|url=http://saintsrlfc.com/player/9|title=Keiron Cunningham profile|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=27 July 2010|access-date=27 July 2010}} and Saints also look to clubs like Wigan St Patricks and Orrell St James in the Wigan area, and Halton Farnworth Hornets in the Widnes area for youth talent. However, a criticism of the St Helens scouting system is that they tend not to look at players beyond the junior ages (6–16 years old), and talented players who continue into amateur rugby tend to be signed very rarely. File:AmateurRugbyU11.jpg|[[Blackbrook A.R.L.F.C.|Blackbrook Royals]] have a large contingent of players in St Helens first team squad. File:Keiron Cunningham.JPG|[[Keiron Cunningham]], perhaps the prime example of St Helens deep roots in junior rugby ===Feeder clubs=== Without having strict feeder sides, such as the system that exists in Australasia, St Helens have, in the last 20 years, particularly with the inception of the dual—registration scheme in 2009, built up partnerships with [[Co-operative Championship]] sides like [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]] and [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2515|title=Emmitt: Dual System Is Giving Me Confidence|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=12 May 2010|access-date=31 July 2010}} St Helens have also been known to loan fringe players to "less strong" Super League sides such as [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] and [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]] and previously Widnes. St Helens have also forged links with the [[Canada Rugby League]] (CRL) and their team [[Toronto City Saints]], who have adopted the popular piece of St Helens insignia in their crest.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2797|title=Toronto Becomes Second CRL Domestic Club|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=3 September 2010|access-date=6 October 2010}} Outside of rugby league, St Helens have forged partnerships with [[British Basketball League]] side [[Mersey Tigers]],{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2273|title=Saints Join Forces With Everton Tigers|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=1 February 2010|access-date=22 October 2010}} and [[English Premier Ice Hockey League]] team [[Manchester Phoenix]].{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2294|title=Saints Team Up With Manchester Phoenix|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=12 February 2010|access-date=22 October 2010}} ==Supporters== [[File:St Helens RFC Fans.jpg|thumb|right|300px|St Helens fans in Perpignan 2016]] St Helens are one of the best supported teams in Super League, averaging 11,543 according to 2014 figures. Situated, prior to their recent move to Widnes, in [[Eccleston, Merseyside|Eccleston and Thatto Heath]], a lot of support naturally derives from that area. Many strongholds of support also come from the Eastern side of the town; areas like [[Blackbrook, St Helens|Blackbrook]], [[Haydock]], and [[Parr, Merseyside|Parr]]. However, there exists considerable support towards [[Newton-le-Willows|Newton]], [[Billinge, Merseyside|Billinge]] and [[Ashton-in-Makerfield|Ashton]] also. Their support is also not restricted to the town, with bases in [[Liverpool]], [[Manchester]], [[Birmingham]], [[Northamptonshire]], [[Southern England|the South]] and [[North Wales]]. The club also have their own supporters clubs, one for the club itself which has folded,{{cite web|url=http://www.originalsaintssupportersclub.co.uk/|title=Original St Helens R.L.F.C.Supporters Club|publisher=originalsaintssupportersclub.co.uk|date=2 March 2011|access-date=2 March 2011|archive-date=20 May 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130520221356/http://originalsaintssupportersclub.co.uk/|url-status=dead}} and one for fans in the South.{{cite web|url=http://www.imperialexiles.org/meridian/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110726174127/http://www.imperialexiles.org/meridian/|url-status=usurped|archive-date=26 July 2011|title=Imperial Exiles|publisher=Imperial Exiles|date=2 March 2011|access-date=2 March 2011}} International, as well as national support is also strong with Saints. A recent survey showed fan bases in Australia, the United States, France, Spain, Ireland, Scotland, Canada, the Middle East, Chile and Greece. The club also have something of a fan base forming in the Far East, with fans from Singapore and Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/1928|title=Is This Saints' Most Travelled fan?|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=14 July 2009|access-date=21 November 2010}}{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/1951|title=Thanks From Katz...|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=23 July Saints|publisher=bbc.co.uk|date=3 February 2005|access-date=1 November 2010}} ==Colours and badge== ===Colours=== In their rugby football days and early years as a rugby league club, St Helens wore a vertically striped blue and white jersey with blue shorts and socks. In their initial period at Knowsley Road, St Helens wore a similar jersey, but the stripes were horizontal, and the colours were blue and grey. These colours however were dropped in favour of a more traditional to today, red and white design. The jersey had a single broad red horizontal band, on a white background, and was used until 1961. {{col-begin-small}} {{col-4}} {{Football kit | pattern_la = _white_stripes | pattern_b = _whitestripes | pattern_ra = _white_stripes | leftarm = 0000FF | body = 0000FF | rightarm = 0000FF | shorts = 0000FF | socks = 0000FF | title = Very early kit }} {{col-4}} {{Football kit | pattern_la = _red_hoops | pattern_b = _redhorizontal | pattern_ra = _red_hoops | leftarm = FFFFFF | body = FFFFFF | rightarm = FFFFFF | shorts = FFFFFF | socks = FF0000 | title = Kit until 1961 }} {{col-4}} {{Football kit | pattern_la = _redborder | pattern_b = _Vwidered | pattern_ra = _redborder | leftarm = FFFFFF | body = ffffff | rightarm = FFFFFF | shorts = FFFFFF | socks = FFFFFF | title = 1961–present }} {{col-end}}[[File:St. Helens heritage jersey 2010.svg|thumb|100px|St Helens heritage jersey, first used in 1890.]]In the 1961 Challenge Cup final against Wigan, the strip still seen today—the famous red V—was first utilised. In 1981, the club changed colours again, albeit temporarily, when [[Umbro]] designed and manufactured a [[France national rugby league team|French]]—style jersey of blue, with a white and red V. These colours were donned for two years, before the traditional colours were reverted to. 1985 saw the first jersey that was supported by a visible sponsor—St Helens Glass. Umbro were once again the designers of this varsity blue jersey with a royal blue vee—shaped chevron, a kit similar to the away kit of 2008. This was also the first jersey to feature the famous stickman of St Helens insignia. The 1985 New Zealand tour jersey was similar to the home shirt of 2009, sponsored also by [[Pilkington]]. Between 1991 and 1994, a somewhat controversial and odd{{According to whom|date=April 2017}} design of jersey was employed, where the chevron that St Helens had now adopted in place of the traditional V finished three—quarters of the way down the shirt. Umbro remained the club's kit suppliers until 1994, when Stag took over. Their jerseys were far more lightweight than what they had replaced, and they chose to revert to a more conventional fashion of red V in their designs. The kit used first by the club was reverted to during their centenary year in 1995. {{col-begin-small}} {{col-4}} {{Football kit | pattern_la = _sthelens1991h | pattern_b = _sthelens1991h | pattern_ra = _sthelens1991h | leftarm = ffffff | body = ffffff | rightarm = ffffff | shorts = ffffff | socks = ffffff | title = 1991/92 kit |pattern_sh=_redsides|pattern_so=_redstripe}} {{col-4}} {{Football kit | pattern_la = _sthelens2026h | pattern_b = _sthelens2026h | pattern_ra = _sthelens2026h | leftarm = FFFFFF | body = FFFFFF | rightarm = FFFFFF | shorts = FFFFFF | socks = FF0000 | title = 1996 kit
(used again in 2026) |pattern_sh=_sthelens2026h|pattern_so=_sthelens2026h}} {{col-4}} {{Football kit | pattern_la = _sthelens1997h | pattern_b = _sthelens1997h | pattern_ra = _sthelens1997h | leftarm = 000000 | body = 000000 | rightarm = 000000 | shorts = 000000 | socks = ffffff | title = 1997 kit |pattern_sh=_sthelens1997h|pattern_so=_sthelens1997h}} {{col-end}} In 1997, yet another change of design that was controversial{{According to whom|date=April 2017}} with the fans, a kit that featured a white drop—down V with a black and red mesh design at the bottom of the jersey. This was [[Mizuno Corp.|Mizuno]]'s first design, taking over from Stag. The traditional red V was once more reverted to, after the controversial design, with a more circular design used for the [[Super League IV|1999 season]]. In 2000, a more regular V was seen again. In 2003, a triple—V design was seen, and the first to be used by long—serving kit designers [[Puma AG]]. 2004 saw a double curvy red V used, before, in 2005, yet again tradition was reverted to in the design and this design was used until 2009. The away strip used in 2005 was the famous blue strip with a dark blue V. 2009 saw the long red V of 1985's design appear again, before, in 2010, a casual thin red V was seen. In 2010, the club used the first kit they played a rugby league game design as a commemorative strip, to celebrate their 110th and final year at the ground.[http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php Saints Heritage Society], see General Pages and Shirt Tales. Retrieved 20 October 2010. 2011 saw the Puma contract expire, and Australian manufacturing giants [[International Sports Clothing|ISC]] take over the making of the jerseys.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2738|title=Saints Agree Sportswear Partnership With ISC|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=9 August 2010|access-date=23 October 2010}} This strip saw a somewhat shorter red vee, compared to the ventures of the design in 2009 and '10.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2904|title=Record Pre-Orders For New Home Shirt|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=25 November 2010|access-date=26 November 2010}} ===Badge=== [[File:St Helens RLFC pre 2012.png|thumb|The badge used by the club between 1997 and 2010.]] [[File:St Helens Crest pre-1997.png|thumb|The old crest used by the club before 1997. This version was also worn in the 2026 season.]] The club's jerseys were initially adorned by the town's coat of arms. This was utilised until 1985. However, as Saints became a more national institution, they decided that a more recognisable badge needed to be adopted. The 1985 season therefore saw the famous stickman of St Helens first used. This was used as the main jersey emblem until 1991, when the St Helens sports club emblem, not dissimilar to the coat of arms used previously, was seen. This was used until 1996, when, with the implement of Super League into the British rugby league calendar, Saints chose to display a badge that featured an overlapping "S" and "H" in red, with the club's name around the border. This was used until 2010. The club decided to unofficially rebrand to "Saints RL", to coincide with them leaving Knowsley Road, and going "on the road" to Widnes for a season. The new crest was in the shape of a shield, and featured the over lapping "S" and "H" that the previous logo featured, but also displayed a red vee within the design, and "Saints RL" in upper case lettering at the top of the shield. This new crest drew criticism from large sections of the St Helens faithful, who were afraid of the club losing its connection to the town to attract a wider fan base.{{cite web|url=http://www.sthelensstar.co.uk/news/8674281.There___s_no_identity_crisis_say_Saints/|title=There's no identity crisis say Saints|work=St Helens Star|date=18 November 2010|access-date=1 December 2010}} ===Kit manufacturers and sponsors=== Since 2015 St Helens' kit has been supplied by [[O'Neills]].{{Cite web |title=Saints sign deal with O'Neill's Sportswear |url=https://www.sthelensstar.co.uk/sport/11551824.saints-sign-deal-with-oneills-sportswear/ |access-date=2022-12-11 |website=St Helens Star |date=22 October 2014 |language=en}} Previous suppliers include [[Umbro]] (1986–94), Stag (1995–96), [[Mizuno Corporation]] (1997–99), Y2K (2000), Exito (2001–02), [[Puma SE|Puma]] (2003–10), and [[International Sports Clothing|ISC]] (2011–14). Their current main shirt sponsor is [[Home Bargains]].{{Cite web |last=Cultivate |date=2021-11-18 |title=Champion Retailer Home Bargains to Become Saints Principal Shirt Partner |url=https://www.saintsrlfc.com/2021/11/18/champion-retailer-home-bargains-to-become-saints-principal-shirt-partner/ |access-date=2022-12-11 |website=St.Helens R.F.C. |language=en-GB}} Previous main sponsors have included St Helens Glass (1986–87, 2000–01), [[McEwan's Lager]] (1987–98), [[John Smith's]] (1999), Caledonia (2002), Comodo (2003), All Sports (2004–05), Earth money (2006–07), [[Pilkington]] Activ (2008–09), Frontline (2010), Medicash (2011), [[Typhoo]] (2012–2016), RCMA Group (2017), and [[Cash Converters]]{{Cite web |title=Cash Converters become Saints' chief shirt sponsor |url=https://www.sthelensstar.co.uk/sport/15637687.cash-converters-become-saints-chief-shirt-sponsor/ |access-date=2022-12-11 |website=St Helens Star |date=2 November 2017 |language=en}} (2018–2021). St. Helens were also sponsored by [[Gillette]]{{Cite web |title=Dubai Sevens St Helens Pictures and Photos – Getty Images |url=https://www.gettyimages.co.uk/photos/dubai-sevens-st-helens?family=editorial&assettype=image&sort=best&phrase=dubai%20sevens%20st%20helens |access-date=2022-12-11 |website=Gettyimages.co.uk}} for a one-off appearance at the 2004 [[Dubai Sevens|Dubai Rugby 7s]] invitational.{{Cite web |last=Echo |first=Liverpool |date=2004-12-07 |title=Saints were in Sevens heaven |url=https://www.liverpoolecho.co.uk/sport/other-sport/saints-were-in-sevens-heaven-3536926 |access-date=2022-12-11 |website=Liverpool Echo |language=en}} See Below: {| class="wikitable" !Year !Sponsor !Manufacturer |- |1986–87 |St Helens Glass | rowspan="2" |Umbro |- |1988–94 | rowspan="3" |McEwan's Lager |- |1995–96 |Stag |- |1997–98 | rowspan="2" |Mizuno |- |1999 |John Smith's |- |2000 | rowspan="2" |St Helens Glass |Y2K |- |2001 | rowspan="2" |Excito |- |2002 |Caledonia |- |2003 |Comodo | rowspan="5" |Puma |- |2004–05 |All:Sports |- |2006–07 |Earth Money |- |2008–09 |Pilkington Activ |- |2010 |Frontline |- |2011 |Medicash | rowspan="2" |ISC |- |2012–14 | rowspan="2" |Typhoo |- |2015–16 | rowspan="4" |O'Neills |- |2017 |RCMA Group |- |2018–21 |Cash Converters |- |2022–26 |Home Bargains |} ==Mascots== [[File:Mascot St Helens.JPG|thumb|left|Boots before the [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] derby in [[Super League XIV|2009]]]] During the [[Super League]] era, the participating teams have adopted mascots and nicknames usually in alliteration with the name of their home town. Initially, the St Helens mascots were Bernard and Bernadette, [[St. Bernard (dog)|St Bernard dogs]]; depicting something of a married couple with their on-field humorous antics. However, in 2009, the mascots changed to Boots and Bernard; happy and angry masculine characters. Bernard doesn't appear as often as Boots, with Boots being a more child-friendly image for the club, while Bernard retains the 'seriousness' of the mascots role to the club.{{cite web|url=http://saintsrlfc.com/news/1752|title=St Bernard Steps Up to the Challenge|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=6 May 2009|access-date=26 July 2010}} ==Stadia== {{further|Knowsley Road|BrewDog Stadium}} {{multiple image | direction = vertical | width = 200 | image1 = Knowsley Road St Helens.jpg | caption1 = [[Knowsley Road]] from the Away End view }} St Helens' former stadium was [[Knowsley Road]], renamed in 2008 to the GPW Recruitment Stadium for sponsorship reasons. Prior to this, in particular when St Helens were playing simply rugby football, they shared the City Ground with [[Pilkington Recs|St Helens Recs]]. They left this stadium in 1890 for Knowsley Road and played there for 120 years. The ground at the time of its construction was considered modern, with one seated stand, and three standing areas that could, prior to strict safety regulations set in place, hold up to 40,000 people, shown by their record home crowd of 35,695 against Wigan in 1949. In their first match at the new ground, St Helens beat Manchester Rangers, played under rugby football rules. After the great schism of 1895, and St Helens joining the NRFU, their first game at Knowsley Road under traditional rugby league rules was against Rochdale, in front of 3,000 spectators. Over the years, however, age took its toll on the ground. Despite the big names like Meninga, van Vollenhoven, Lyon etc. coming to the club, it was constantly argued that, unlike other big clubs, St Helens did not have the stadium to suit their on-field talent. In 2006, the ground was treated to something of a makeover, after financial input from St Helens big money sponsors Earth Money. This aided the ground in gaining international rugby, with a fixture between [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] and [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] being held there in 2006. In 2008, St Helens were given a warning letter from the RFL, as a result of the ensuing licensing system that was to be introduced into Super League in 2009, stating that the quality of their current stadium was too poor for the expected quality of a licence in the league.{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=14771|title=5 Clubs Issued With Licensing Letters|publisher=superleague.co.uk|date=22 July 2009|access-date=7 August 2009|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090723201304/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=14771|archive-date=23 July 2009}} Thankfully for Saints, they were permitted by the council to begin construction on a [[Langtree Park|new ground]], and confirmed that they would move away from Knowsley Road in 2011.{{cite web|url=http://www.sthelensstadium.com/default.htm|title=St Helens RLFC New Stadium|publisher=sthelensstadium.com|date=15 July 2009|access-date=14 July 2009|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090709085620/http://www.sthelensstadium.com/default.htm|archive-date=9 July 2009}} It was announced that the new complex, to be built on an unused glass site in Peasley Cross, was to feature at least 12,000 seats, a large car park, and a [[Tesco]] store next to the ground. In addition to this, an iconic bridge, directly linking the ground to the town centre went under construction on 9 August 2010.{{cite web|url=http://www.saintsrlfc.com/news/2741|title=Work Begins on Bridge|publisher=saintsrlfc.com|date=9 August 2010|access-date=9 August 2010}} Whether the stadium would be ready for the beginning of the [[Super League XVI|2011 season]] was always uncertain, and grounds such as [[Leigh Sports Village]] were suggested to home Saints for the period between. However, the [[Halton Stadium]], [[Widnes]] was the chosen venue for Saints for the 2011 season, and they played all of their home games there, before permanently relocating in 2012.{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12206_6268665,00.html|title=Saints shifting to Widnes|publisher=skysports.com|date=18 July 2010|access-date=18 July 2010}} Saints currently play at the [[BrewDog Stadium]]. ==2026 squad== {{St Helens R.F.C. current squad}} ==2026 transfers== {{unreferenced|section|date=October 2025}} ===Transfers In=== {| class="wikitable" |- !
